data.title: TSLZ: A Tactical Tool Waiting In The Wings
data.description:
In October 2023, T-Rex 2X Inverse Tesla Daily Target ETF (BATS:TSLZ) was the first single-stock 2X inverse leveraged ETF to debut on U.S. bourses. That gives us about a year and nine months of performance data, but rather than looking at long-term returns right away, it is more prudent to explore how the daily reset on this ETF works, and how you can possibly use it as a vehicle to take advantage of declines in the underlying asset, which is Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) stock.

I covered TSLZ back in March of this year. At the time, I recommended it as a downside capture tool for TSLA shareholders. I still do, but I think now is a far more appropriate time to learn how to use this quasi-shorting tool on TSLA. The bond between Trump and Musk is broken, and in my view, it is not beneath Trump to undertake a personal vendetta against his erstwhile 'best bud'. Case in point: The White House has now directed NASA and the Department of Defense to "review" SpaceX contracts, with a view to possibly limiting its role in the Golden Dome.

That aside, Tesla has been struggling on several fronts lately: sales slumps across multiple markets, general animosity toward the actions taken during his short-lived bureaucratic stint, and ongoing challenges in the China supply chain equation have all contributed to poor Q1 performance for the world's most iconic EV brand.

Likelihood of a Decline in TSLA Stock

Honestly speaking, my Sell rating on TSLA did not hold up too well, but I think that was more a case of misplaced optimism on the part of bullishly entrenched shareholders. The stock was at $270 or so when I last covered TSLZ, surging to over $360 in a matter of weeks. Of late, it has pulled back to around $320 to $325 and seems to be holding steady, relatively speaking. There is no doubt that it has always been a volatile stock, so that is not about to change, but I think investors are growing increasingly impatient for Magician Musk to pull the next rabbit out of his hat.

This is where I turn contrarian, as I point out the problem, which is that the current scenario makes TSLZ a poor tool to capture TSLA downside, at least for the time being. Regardless, investors should be prepared for the worst. We shall see why, and we shall also see how the stock's inherent volatility can be used to generate handsome returns whenever TSLA hits a downswing. For now, it seems unlikely that TSLA will continue to decline. If at all, it will purely be based on fundamentals. In itself, that is an oddity for a company that has long been valued based on promises made by the CEO.

What is Likely to Happen with TSLA?

Contrary to most investors' expectations for TSLA to have a further downside, I do not think that will happen in the short term. First, we shall look at what the experts think.

Seeking Alpha's Quant rating for TSLA has been a Hold for the longest time. Since the pandemic struck, there has only been a very brief time when Quant carried a Strong Buy rating.

Much of this had to do with the Revisions grade, where Street analysts have swung widely between A+ and F, depending on how earnings trended at those times. The other factor grade that has been responsible for the overall Hold over the last five years has been Growth.

Together, these two factor grades have kept Quant from rating Tesla a Buy or a Strong Buy. Right now, it is TSLA's valuation and downward revisions by analysts that are maintaining the Hold rating. Valuation has always been a challenge with this stock, since it typically factors in new products or features that have yet to hit the market or are based on Musk's tweeted/x-ed commitments. Indeed, it has rarely moved above an F since Quant started covering it in August 2019; you can see this if you choose the 3-year Quant rating option on this page.

Wall Street ratings, as captured on Seeking Alpha, are a similar story. Analysts on the Street have never rated it higher than a Buy, and even that was only when TSLA traded at under $290 between April and August 2023.

Even Seeking Alpha analysts haven't been very optimistic about the stock. As a matter of fact, there were several periods when TSLA was rated a Sell.

The broad theme seems to be a Hold on the stock, and for various reasons. I have rated Tesla a Sell in the past, but there is something about the stock that has defied expert opinion over the past five years, during which period it has gained over 400%. Said differently, the downswings appear to be the effect of news, while upswings seem to be the broad bullish view of Tesla's mostly-next-generation products and features like full self-driving, robots, robotaxis, and the like - with the end result being the stock keeps moving upward.

If that changes, and that is something that can easily happen overnight, then TSLZ would be the perfect tool to take advantage of it.

How to Play TSLZ on TSLA Downswings

Since TSLZ is leveraged to capture twice the downside of TSLA, it is logical to assume that investors should only use it as a tactical tool. This is not intended to be a buy and hold security; on the contrary because of the daily reset, it is not prudent to hold it for longer than a few days, at the most.

The leverage itself is achieved through the use of total return swaps, and is structured in a way that allows the fund to make twice the amount that TSLA loses on any given trading day.

Buying these ETF shares is equal to participating in this leveraged play. Theoretically, if TSLA drops by an X amount, TSLZ will increase in value by 2X. On every trading day when TSLA closes lower than it opens is a gain day for the ETF's shares. This is exactly why it is a risky play because that leverage works in the opposite direction when TSLA gains on a given trading day.

The big risk here is that the ETF resets daily, and this is an important aspect of leveraged ETFs one should fully understand and appreciate. Suffice it to know that holding the fund for prolonged periods could potentially lead to something known as slippage or decay, and that is the direct result of the leverage applied to the ETF's performance. It works the same way for unleveraged products as well, but the effect is amplified 2X with this particular class of ETFs.

So, how does one know when to invest in TSLZ? Although the mechanics of the total return swaps are complex, one can better understand their results by studying the price momentum of the underlying TSLA asset. Let us look at an example of how TSLZ moves against a declining TSLA.

Between TSLA's peak in December 2024 and the trough in March 2025, it lost a little more than 50 percent of its market capitalization. Look at how much TSLZ gained in that time, and this despite the decay we talked about earlier. The point being that in a steady downtrend (for TSLA), TSLZ has the potential to give investors a lot more than the 2X inverse returns that the ETF aims to achieve.

I know this goes against what I said earlier about not holding this instrument for more than a day or so, and I maintain that because nobody can predict a prolonged downturn; more importantly, nobody can predict when it might reverse and wipe out all or a significant portion of their gains until then.

What one can do is use momentum indicators such as RSI to determine the best entry and exit points for TSLZ, the tactic being to buy when TSLA is in overbought territory and sell before it hits oversold.

This relatively simple strategy can allow you to repeatedly gain significant returns in the long run.

Is it Worth the Risk?

That is the question to ask yourself because I have no idea what your risk tolerance level is. Just keep in mind that the losses made on a TSLZ trade are very hard to reverse - again, due to the daily reset.

My opinion is that TSLA shareholders would do well to use this as a sort of hedge to offset declines that last more than a day, and that is something that momentum indicators like RSI should help you do.

As for my recommendation, a Hold is the only appropriate one as far as I am concerned. Although the risk is elevated, so is the reward.

data.title: PayPal: Stablecoin Will Power Growth
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

Shares of PayPal (NASDAQ:PYPL) have had a rough first half of 2025. Year-to-date, shares of the financial technology company are down nearly 17%. With the rise of stablecoins the reasons for PayPal’s underperformance has been due to fears that Apple Pay and Shopify are encroaching on PayPal’s checkout share along with stablecoins encroaching on their payment processing fees.

With this, however, I really believe that these concerns are overstated. PayPal has entrenched merchant relationships and high-conversion funnels.

I want to focus on management’s “win checkout” push for 2025 - particularly the Fastlane one-click guest checkout launched in early 2024, which I wrote about extensively during my last piece of coverage on PayPal. I view this as a direct effort to streamline purchases and defend PayPal’s core franchise.

I believe that PayPal’s vast network of over 400 million accounts between both consumers and merchants creates durable scale advantages that are hard for new entrants like Apple Pay and Shopify to replicate. In my opinion, there is promising optionality in PayPal’s growth stories - most notably crypto - highlighted by the 2023 rollout of the PYUSD stablecoin, which could help lower transaction costs and open new rails for payments. With legislation moving through Washington DC that promises regulations for Stablecoins, this could be a huge catalyst. Paypal should benefit.

With this, I continue to believe shares are a strong buy.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

Since I published my last coverage on PayPal, shares of the company dipped by as much as 21%, but they have since rebounded, and they’re down just about 2% since late February when I last published coverage on PayPal. I think most of this recent selloff has been driven by Apple Pay and Shopify fears, echoing mid-2022 worries that ultimately proved unfounded when Apple integrated PayPal as a checkout option.

I’ve seen anecdotal bear arguments gain some traction, but I think these are isolated and are definitely not reflective of PayPal’s broad merchant base.

Something that keeps me bullish on PayPal heading into the rest of the year is CEO Alex Chriss relentless focus on winning back market share as his top priority. Fastlane is a big part of this story.

With this, early metrics continue to reassure me. As noted on the Q4 call:

75% of Fastlane consumers are new or dormant PayPal users. This means that Fastlane not only improves conversion for our merchants, but also introduces more shoppers to PayPal and enables us to reengage in active users.

With the growth of their stablecoin + stronger regulatory backing in DC for stablecoin, I think this makes now a perfect time to revisit PayPal. I believe the recent pullback is just short-term noise. I think the bull case is accelerating.

Stablecoin Deep Dive

To understand why Stablecoins are a big deal for PayPal, I want to focus on PayPal USD and why this is such a big development for PayPal. PayPal USD (or PYUSD) was launched in August 2023 as the first US-backed stablecoin from a major fintech company. The stablecoin was issued via Paxos Trust, is fully collateralized by dollar deposits and U.S. treasuries, and is pegged 1:1 to the U.S. dollar.

With growing adoption (and a more favorable environment in DC) we’ve seen a trend begin to develop where stablecoins are being brought into mainstream payments. PYUSD is being integrated across both PayPal and Venmo for P2P transfers, Xoom remittances, and third-party wallet payments. The goal here is to leverage blockchain rails to undercut card network fees and settlement times.

I think PayPal’s 400 million+ user base and merchant integrations give PYUSD a huge distribution edge in both commerce use cases and remittances - both of which are situations where crypto-native stablecoins have struggled to gain traction.

To add to this, in April 2025, PayPal partnered with Coinbase to waive fees on PYUSD buys and sells and enable one-click conversion to USD. In the future, I expect the roadmap to enable merchants to settle directly in PYUSD. This could be huge and help eliminate fees with cross-border transactions.

PayPal plans to roll out a 3.7% APY yield on PYUSD balances in PayPal and Venmo wallets starting in the summer. What this means is that it will make holding PYUSD materially more attractive than cash or rival stablecoins.

I view PYUSD as a catalyst for seriously accelerating the amount of cash PayPal holds on its balance sheet that it can generate interest on. In a way, PayPal becomes closer to a bank, which could have huge implications on net income margins.

The potential for this market could be huge, with US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent estimating that the TAM of stablecoins could exceed $2 Trillion in just a few years.

If Paypal grabbed 20% market share with its stablecoin ($400 billion) and then make a 1% net income margin spread on it ($4 billion) this could go a long way to increase their bottom line.

Valuation

Currently, PayPal has a forward Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 13.88. This is a 33.85% premium to fintech/financial peers, with the sector median currently sitting at 10.37. However, this 13.88 P/E ratio by PayPal is more than 51% below the company’s five-year average of 28.66. Seeking Alpha Quant gives PayPal a grade of a D+ on this metric.

PayPal’s forward price-to-book ratio of 3.22 is almost 196% higher than the sector median of 1.09. Meanwhile, similar to their forward price-to-earnings ratio, this mark is about 50% below the five-year average for PayPal. The company earns a grade of a D- on this metric. In my opinion, these two metrics reflect PayPal’s profitability. The market still values the company at a higher multiple than a traditional bank (more tech savvy) but not as high as other fintech leaders.

Interestingly, PayPal’s forward projected operating cash flow growth rate of 20.12% is more than double the sector median of 10.04%. This is given a grade of an B+ by Seeking Alpha Quant.

In essence, the company trades at a 33.85% premium to the sector median on a forward P/E ratio yet has forward cashflow growth that is projected to be more than double the sector median over the next 12 months (4 quarters).

In essence, I think the company’s forward P/E ratio should be at least a 60% premium to the sector median given this well above sector median growth.

If we saw their P/E premium expand to a 60% premium, this would represent 77.25% upside from here.

We get this upside by taking the projected premium (60% premium) and dividing it by the current premium (33.85%).

Frankly, I think even this 60% premium may turn out to be conservative. If we price in an additional $4 billion in net income from a 20% market share in the stablecoin market, I think this will make their cash flow growth even stronger than expected.

Risks

PayPal (despite their share price performance) should be a winner from the stablecoin revolution. That doesn’t mean they are risk free.

The biggest risk from the stablecoin revolution, in my opinion, is the intense fintech competition. I see Stripe’s global stablecoin push (their Bridge acquisition) and Apple/Shopify checkout integrations as credible threats to PayPal’s market share and fee-take rates.

Another risk I see is how ingrained some of the early movers are. It’s clear to me that USDC and USDT have cemented themselves as leaders. Switching would require clear benefits (their account yield of 3.7% APY may accomplish this). The other benefit is that PayPal is going down the regulatory-sanctioned route for stablecoins. This means that as USDC and USDT (run by Tether) are subject to more audits, we should see the benefits of building a stablecoin with regulations in mind as a key strategic edge.

There have been industry rumors that some stablecoins like Tether are not 1:1 backed by liquid assets. What we are about to find (thanks to the stablecoin act called the GENIUS Act) is that stablecoins are about to become a market where participants (stablecoin issuers) ask for regulatory permission.

Not regulatory forgiveness. Most of finance works this way. As stablecoins move this way as well, PayPal should benefit.

Bottom Line

While shares have slumped year to date I remain confident in PayPal. I think they’ve established themselves as a trusted brand with high-conversion checkout funnels and two-sided network effects. I think these things underpin what I think is its durable digital-payments leadership and path to serious growth in the stablecoin space.

I view PYUSD as a long-term growth engine. If mainstream stablecoin usage takes off within PayPal’s ecosystem, it could meaningfully lower processing costs and deepen engagement. I think most of the bearish concerns are coming from Apple/Shopify/Braintree headwinds, but I think all of these are manageable and are largely priced in. This sets a low bar for positive surprises in 2025-26. I think Stable coins are the biggest part of the bull thesis. Stablecoins have the opportunity to be a $4 billion lift to net income alone just on effective net interest income margins.

I think the valuation is supported by really durable cash flow, which makes for a scenario where investors effectively get optional upside from new initiatives “for free” on top of the stable core business. If management can successfully execute on checkout innovation and stablecoin adoption, I still consider PayPal to be a strong-buy with asymmetric upside over the next 12-24 months. The market appears to be irrational. This should be a gift to investors who are patient.

data.title: The Fed Fires A Quiet Warning Shot
data.description:
Introduction

It was no surprise to investors that the Federal Reserve's FOMC (Federal Reserve Open Market Committee) which dictates short-term interest rates held rates steady at 4.25-4.50% at their June 18th meeting.

Futures markets had been predicting this for months, and even leading up to the day, expected the tail case of the meeting to be to the upside on rates, not the downside. The market believed that, if the Fed wasn't going to hold rates, it was more likely they raise than cut. That movement happened just before the Fed meeting, on the same day, and surprised me.

That is to say that the market still believes in the Fed ratcheting rates down over time, with a near 15% chance to cut in July, based on current futures expectations, though this is likely to change as the days go by, and we get more economic data releases.

What surprised me is how the statement changed; its tone is far more dovish than I expected, even if rates were held and the hawkish policy stance continues, at least until the next meeting in July.

What Changed In the FOMC Statement

For those of you who haven't seen this version of the statement before, here is how to read it, a la CNBC, which publishes this version:

Text removed from the May statement is in red with a horizontal line through the middle. Text appearing for the first time in the new statement is in red and underlined. Black text appears in both statements.

The first thing of note for me comes with the replacement of this line:

Uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further.

For this line:

Uncertainty about the economic outlook has diminished but remains elevated.

This was paired with the removal of this line in the following sentence:

[The FOMC]...judges that the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen.

Of course, in taking questions about this change of language, Powell reiterated his usual stance: it's all dependent on data, and "diminished but remains elevated" is not "gone".

These changes were very dovish, potentially indicating a shift in the Fed's policy. If all I saw from the Fed was this statement, I would think rate cuts start next month.

That's not all the Fed action that happened, though.

The dovish rhetoric ends, as both Fed chair Powell's presser following the release of the statement, and the projections made by the FOMC committee, were not bullish.

In fact, Powell's comments and the revisions to projections from March (notably pre-Liberation Day) were likely the reason markets fell on the 18th, erasing their gains from the day.

This chart is fascinating because you can see the group think in action. At 2pm, the statement is released and markets rally before the rally is sold, and markets chop until 2:30pm, when Powell starts talking. About ten minutes in, the sell-off begins.

Powell's Presser

The theme of the presser on the 18th was tariffs and their impacts on inflation. Powell said directly that he and the committee believe that inflation from tariffs will be a one-time deal, and will be "transitory," although some of us get a bitter taste in our mouths when we hear that word, flashbacks to the last inflationary episode we had.

To be fair, I agree on this part, so long as the tariff levels are set and held. It's very difficult to know if they will be, with court challenges to the powers, the changing attitude from day to day about the purpose of tariffs (negotiating tool, revenue generator, etc.), and the deadline for the "90 deals in 90 days" still looming next month.

We can see from several answers to questions about tariff-related inflation Powell gave at the presser how much this is on the mind of the FOMC as a real threat to inflation.

Tariff uncertainty peaked in April and has come down since then...

We feel like we're going to learn a great deal more [about the effects of tariffs] over the summer...

We need to see actual data [on tariff impacts] to make better decisions...

We expect a meaningful amount of inflation from tariffs in the coming months...

Everyone that I know is forecasting a meaningful increase in inflation in coming months from tariffs, because someone has to pay for the tariffs...

One of the answers he gave early on in the presser was very telling of Fed policy, where he essentially said that the Fed was waiting on cutting interest rates until they know for certain what the tariff impacts would be. He told a reporter that the rate cut from last year was a hawkish cut (my words, not his) that essentially allowed the Fed to hold rates until they get more clarity from the data.

Following this, he responded to an assertion that the Fed was being very restrictive, where he said that the economy was not acting as if the Fed was being restrictive, and that while rates are high relative to the last five, ten, or twenty years, they are still very low in the grand scheme of history.

Remember that chart at the beginning of the article? Here it is pulled all the way out to 1950.

Of course, the average figure is skewed by the 22% rate high that we saw in the 1980s. I don't believe we'll get to rates like those anytime soon, or maybe ever again, but it should give some context for why Powell believes holding rates where they are as "dry powder" to be able to attack any inflation that does come up the rest of the year.

If you cut rates now and then tariff-related inflation is more insidious or worse than expected, and "modestly restrictive" (Powell's words) monetary policy is needed again, and rates would have to come back up. This kind of back-and-forth is something the Fed tries to avoid, as they prefer stability and stoicism to the move-fast-and-break-things approach taken by the current administration, which is demanding Powell cut rates by 1% or more immediately.

Following the news of no rate cuts, President Trump made remarks about just appointing himself to the Fed in May 2026 when Powell's term expires.

One of the disconnects between the two men and their stances on monetary policy was brought up during the presser when Powell was asked why there was the (hawkish, again my words) cut last year, but no more now that economic data like inflation is even lower than it was then.

Aside from the need to store dry powder, Powell also made a point to iterate and reiterate throughout the presser, that the FOMC is forward-looking, not backward-looking in their monetary policy decisions. While current data is very positive, something I agree with, the Fed is expecting future data to not be as good, so they want the restrictive policy to stay in place for now, likely through the summer.

For reference, here are the "big three" economic indicators and their 10-year charts:

Projections & The Dot Plot

Here is where I saw the most hawkishness from the Fed, and what, I believe, is the "warning shot" fired by them today; the projections regressed in a few areas, especially in the shorter term like this year and next, and less in the long-term picture. Again, tariffs and their impacts are in focus, and that is most likely a short-term, one-time increase in prices that doesn't bring any persistent inflation afterward.

Rate Projections

The current "dot plot," or the collective assessment of where rates should be by all FOMC participants (each person gets a dot for the chart), looks a little ugly, especially in the short-term, where now only two participants see rates falling below 3% in 2026, and all participants believing that rates will stay above 3.5% for the rest of this year.

Participants are split on whether rates should stay above or below 4% through the rest of this year, with 7/19 participants believing we should have no rate cuts this year.

Here is another way to visualize this chart, with the overlay of the March projections for context on this change.

GDP Projections

On the GDP front, we see a reduction of expectations even further from where they were in March, which came as a slight retraction of where they were before that. Particularly, I've been watching the most bullish member of the committee, which has been alone in projecting 2.4-2.5% GDP growth this year, has now fallen; I assume it is the same person now suggesting growth is more likely to come in at 2.0-2.1%. That sentiment is not echoed in the 2026 chart — again, coming back to this one-time inflation episode the Fed is expecting from the dramatic rise of tariff rates.

Aside from that individual, almost everyone fell backward on these metrics.

Unemployment Projections

Regarding unemployment, there is a notable shift in expectations even going as far as lasting into 2027, although this may have less to do with tariffs and more to do with the latest jobs data coming in soft and mixed.

Inflation Projections

Lastly, here is the expectation of inflation; we see a large move up in expectations in both 2025 and 2026, but otherwise, expectations beyond then are fairly stable, with everyone agreeing that long-run inflation looks like 2%, although of course, the Fed believes that (it's part of their mandate and goals).

We can also see this pattern emerge prominently in Core PCE, which excludes food and fuel.

Conclusion

The movements of FOMC expectations, Fed chair Powell's comments, and the changes in the statement all lead me to a singular conclusion: we will experience higher for longer rates whether we like it or not, and likely through the summer. This shouldn't come as a surprise to most folks who have been following the Fed, but this is the loudest they've been so far about the impacts of tariffs on inflation and using that as the reason to not lower rates yet.

The FOMC is in a tough spot, as they don't want to waste their dry powder of high rates that could be lowered (since they don't want to reactively gyrate interest rates if they go too soon for stability's sake) if the economy is still running fine with rates where they are now.

Now that the Fed has been thoroughly politicized by the White House as well, it's a damned if they do, damned if they don't — holding or raising rates would likely be seen as defiance of President Trump, but cutting them would be meeting his demands. Any decision they make now will be a "partisan" decision by those who want to see it that way.

Powell says he's not phased by political rhetoric, but that may not be true of the rest of the FOMC or the person who comes after him next year, especially if it's President Trump himself, though I find that unlikely.

For now, the pressure isn't working, rates are being held even, and are likely to be held that way until September, or maybe even longer than that. All I have left to say is at least the rates on my cash holdings are better than they have been in the last ten years I've been investing.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Albemarle's Lithium Reset: Strong Buy Amid Cyclical Downturn And Structural Rebirth
data.description:
Cyclical Downturn Meets Structural Rebirth: Albemarle's Lithium Reset

We initiate coverage on Albemarle Corporation (NYSE: NYSE:ALB) at Strong Buy with a $125 price target. Albemarle Corporation is the preeminent global supplier of specialty lithium compounds, bromine solutions, and catalysts used in next-generation batteries, basic industrial processes, and energy transition technologies. Underneath the fog of bearish sentiment that clouds the lithium sector, a resilient operating platform and disciplined execution have quietly laid the foundation for a cyclically charged rebound. The disconnect between sentiment and reality couldn't be starker: while the Street continues to extrapolate “lower for longer” pricing in lithium, ALB's multi-pronged ramp (including Kemerton and Meishan as well as utilization optimization at Salar and Qinzhou) has already put the company in position for mid-single-digit (%) volume growth that will exceed consensus expectations for FY25 and FY26. Our top line forecast assumes a $9/kg LCE pricing deck, justified by robust contract coverage and management's demonstrated cost discipline, driving resiliency in profitability even if market pricing remains stuck in neutral.

The Street's deep discount, at just 1.8x EV/Sales almost 50% below global materials peers, simply overlooks the imminent wave of supply-side rationalization, stabilizing macro backdrop, and pathway toward free cash flow breakeven by next year. We underwrite a blended 3.0x EV/Sales on FY26E, which corresponds to an implied 110% equity upside in our base case, and see no risk of further multiple compression unless the global EV adoption story collapses or contract renegotiations are derailed. In our view, the current valuation seems to imply a hard landing that's already unlikely—and as the cyclical storm clouds clear, we see ALB's risk/reward as one of the most asymmetric among global materials names.

Volume Delivers Margin Upside

We see a material disconnect between the lithium price narrative and ALB's volume-led energy storage outlook. Our conviction stems from the scale and quality of its global conversion network, which hit record lithium output across 5 sites in 1Q25, while delivering 36% EBITDA in energy storage (vs peer ES margin compression). Instead of waiting on price normalisation, ALB's strategy is largely throughput expansion at Kemerton, Salar, Qinzhou and Meishan, maximising fixed cost leverage and minimising tolling at higher cost—a supply chain optimisation most cannot replicate at scale.

Importantly, ES volumes are flat in 1Q, which is a quarterly trough seasonally and historically <25% of annual shipments, setting up a material ramp in the following quarters. Our base case follows management's mid-single digit volume growth in FY25E, driven by project ramp-ups and stable contract coverage of 50% of lithium salts volumes on long-term, price floor contracts. With no material contract expiries in 2025 and a customer mix that picks up grid storage demand from regulatory incentives, ALB is uniquely positioned to reprice larger volumes at the stabilised rates as the year progresses.

We model volume leverage outweighing flat pricing, with mid-20%+ ES margins full-year at a conservative $9/kg LCE deck. We see the street underappreciating the magnitude of operating leverage ALB will unlock as volumes shift from 1Q trough to peak seasonal quarters. Execution on the throughput and mix optimisation will drive persistent margin outperformance, cementing ALB as the leader in volume-driven earnings quality on a global basis.

Markets value discipline

We believe the market is materially underestimating ALB's value creation potential, via disciplined cost and capital allocation. We find ALB reached a 90% run-rate on its $350mn cost / productivity improvement program in April, positioning the company to target the high-end of the $300mn-$400mn range over the course of the year. To our view, these actions are far in excess of transitory cost actions with management leading to structural non-headcount spend reductions, supply chain optimization and fixed cost leverage by moving production from tolling to integrated assets.

Deep capital restraint further enhances the company's optionality. We spotlight FY25E capex down >50% y/y establishing a new normalized floor around $700mn-$800mn with the majority of spend directed towards regulatory / maintenance spending, rather than optionality. Capital discipline, combined with working capital improvements and $350mn customer prepayment pushed operating cash conversion to >200% in Q1 (ex-prepayment run-rate 73% already above long-range target of 60%-70%). ALB enters the cyclical trough with an available liquidity position of $3.1bn and Net Debt / Adj. EBITDA of 2.4x protecting investment grade metrics and positioning the company to capitalize on dislocation.

We are convinced ALB's active management of the controllable levers creates an asymmetric risk profile for the company, positioning it to breakeven FCF this year even in a flat $9/kg pricing environment. A sector historically characterized by capital inefficiency and reactive cost actions, we view ALB's playbook as the former with scalability. Disciplined execution is a key driver of MT share shifts and LT equity re-rating in our view, as cyclical sentiment unwinds.

Above-consensus Revenue View Based on Outperformance in Energy-Storage Volumes & Cost Execution

We model Albemarle's FY25E/FY26E revenue of $5.05 bn and $5.59 bn based on a stronger Energy-Storage ramp and stronger mix than the street assumes. Our above-consensus view is predicated on Albemarle's visible execution on project ramps (Salar, Kemerton, Meishan, Qinzhou) adding low- to mid-single digit volumes and absorbing the Chengdu shut-down.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $4.89B 17 $5.38B 24
EBITDA $749.20M 17 $825.26M 24
Net Income $-177.37M 17 $126.51M 24
EPS $-1.51 16 $0.72 12
SG&A Expenses $539.57M 17 $594.35M 24

Critically, we support our base-case on Albemarle's $9/kg LCE deck, which management has recently reiterated is achievable with current contracts coverage. Versus consensus, we model a 5% Energy-Storage volume uplift, reflecting Albemarle's 90% run-rate on cost savings (materially enhancing P&L even in flat pricing). We also bake in faster Specialty pass-through, reflecting Albemarle's mix, with Ketjen catalysts contributing +3% steady growth (in-line with management guidance). While recognizing risk stemming from contract renegotiations/lithium volatility, our scenario analysis and 57% confidence score suggests R/R skewed to modest topline outperformance unless contracts ratchet below $7/kg LCE or ramps under deliver.

Valuation

Our $125 PT is based on a 3.0x forward EV/Sales multiple applied to our FY26E Albemarle revenue estimate of $5.59bn, offering 110% upside as sentiment remains deeply misaligned with execution curves and sector positioning. We view this multiple as appropriately reflecting the strength of Albemarle's double-legged volume growth story — key project expansions alongside best-in-class cost execution — which drives our revenue CAGR of 10.7% through FY26E, 3–4% ahead of consensus.

The 3.0x multiple is comprehensively guarded: below the company's own historical median and peer mean at 3.4x, it still significantly closes the gap to current valuations (recently at 1.8x vs 7th pctile company history and 14th pctile vs global peers), recognizing the current angst but not discarding the mean reversion thesis that the street appear to be missing. This is supported by forward FCF breakeven momentum, macro stabilizers (Chinese demand) and prudential supply-side discipline that argue for a structural re-rating and progressive multiple normalization, not a full 'snap back'. At its core, we see our framework as both point-in-time and catalytic, reflecting conviction in the visibility of earnings potential despite unique R/R in the point in the cycle, but it avoids blue sky.

Risks to our Strong Buy call

Whilst our Strong Buy thesis on Albemarle is predicated on cost discipline, volume ramp, and demand trajectory for lithium being structural, we acknowledge nontrivial risks to our conclusions. Most notably, a "lower for longer" lithium pricing regime remains a non-trivial risk should Chinese converter ramping under renewed state support and/or African supply further neutralizes Western curtailments—should spot weaken meaningfully below our $9/kg LCE base case, forcing a further earnings reset (including OEM recontracting in which case even the immediate floor is broken), both of which would put near-term cashflow recovery and resulting valuation re-normalization out of reach.

Associated with this, risk to execution of the major capacity projects (Kemerton, Salar, Meishan) remain, where meaningful misses on commissioning, ramp, and/or utilization could mean that timely volume offset fails to rescue FY25E–FY26E revenue consensus outperformance. Realised LCE pricing, the pace of consolidation (supply rationalization), and cadence of Albemarle's key projects should be watched as critical early indicators; any meaningful acceleration or delays on these respectively could meaningfully delay the cyclically-driven re-rating—and floor downside as implied by our scenario analysis.

Conclusion

In our view, Albemarle has one of the most asymmetric risk/reward profiles in global materials, with deeply out-of-favor valuation offering a unique entry point ahead of an inevitable cyclical/structural earnings reset. Our confidence is supported by visible project execution and strong contract coverage to allow it to outperform muted consensus expectations as sentiment realigns with operational reality. For sophisticated investors, we see current levels as an opportunity to build exposure ahead of CF and valuation re-rating catalysts – monitoring lithium pricing inflexion and ramp milestones as your path forward.

data.title: Why Lear Is A Significant 'BUY'
data.description:
I have been positive on Lear Corporation (NYSE:LEA) for quite some time. As it happens, it also remains one of the few US-based and dollar-based positions I retain in my portfolio. This share of my investments has dropped to less than 7% as of late, from a high of over 45% 2 years ago. Lear is an automotive supply company that I covered roughly 3 months ago - and where I will argue, even now, that the company has significant upside. I will use the latest results, which we got less than 1.5 months ago, to show you that the upside here in a base, a bearish, and a bullish scenario all imply good returns for the long term. While risks exist, I view them as "calculable".

Let's therefore see what we have here. You can find my last coverage of the company here. While the company has underperformed the S&P 500, it has done so at a minimal enough rate that I would consider this a "rounding error", not easy to see whether it's really a clear company underperformance, or just the market volatility at the time.

Lear is, in fact, one of several automotive supply stocks that I have increased coverage on in 2025, including Autoliv (ALV) as well as Magna (MGA). Why?

Because I see incredible value in the automotive supply industry, I recently covered BMW (OTC:BMWKY) (OTCPK:BAMXF) as well, so I invest across the entire value chain. It might not be as exciting an investment as Tesla (TSLA), to be sure, but given the dividend and return potential and what I view as better visibility, I believe it to be a preferable one if you're a value-oriented investor such as myself.

Lear - a visible 15%+ annualized upside, enhanced by recent Q1 '25 returns

Let's straight away start with recent results. Looking at both Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts (in this context, that's pretty much me, given that other analysts aside from me have had limited coverage since October 2024), are bullish - Quant is negative, likely due to revisions, meaning recent results and potential trends. That stance is also understandable - it's, after all, been the correct stance for going on at least a year at this point.

While Q1 '25 provides no clear upside in immediate profitability or improvement in trends, what it does is showcase the company's profitability and earnings ability In what is perhaps the most complex and difficult automotive situation/market in 1-3 decades. And despite this situation, the company's sales are above $22B on an NTM basis, operating earnings are above $250M for the quarter, and adjusted earnings remain above $3.1/share.

Part of the reason I like Lear is its complete independence, in some areas, from powertrains and technology. This is because it works in things like seating, which we'll need regardless of how cars are driven or even who operates them. Even driverless cars will need seating - and Lear is a market leader in seating. The company even expanded the leadership in the subsegment, and improved margins in the segment as well, going so far as to acquire operating control of a Chinese JV that supplies seating to the currently vastly popular BYD Han L and Tai 3 models.

These trends go to show you that management knows what it is doing - added to by the fact that Lear was awarded programs yet again, one with Swedish safety leader Volvo (OTCPK:VOLAF)(OTCPK:VOLVF) and one with Hyundai, both for innovative technologies in comfort and ergonomic segments with seating and steering.

The company was further awarded seat programs, complete ones, for the major domestic automotive companies BYD, FAW, and XPeng, all of which will be using Lear seating. Its development encourages me to keep investing in Lear.

Aside from seating, it works in wiring and e-systems, and these are working well also. There's a reason I mentioned investing in BMW - BMW awarded Lear its business in China, the third major BMW/Lear cooperation, and the first in China here. While Lear isn't being 100% transparent about its award for a second-generation battery tech unit, this has also been awarded.

These positive trends also resulted in the company continuing its buyback program, taking advantage of market weakness in the share price to buy back $25M worth of shares.

When I last wrote about the company, a major question was tariffs - so let's get into the meat of it as things now stand towards the end of June. The company imports most of its products and components from either Canadian or Mexican plants in. It also has indirect exposure through European sales on vehicles that are exported, the same as with Asian vehicles, such as Korean/Japanese. Part of the reason I am heavily discounting Lear is that there's very little the company can do to improve this short term. It's planning some manufacturing adjustments and changes, but much of its energy is going to discussions. It's worth mentioning that the tariff situation doesn't seem to be spiraling out of control as much as it did in April, or even when these results were posted about a month ago, but it's still something to look at given Lear's overall exposure. I would continue to characterize the tariff question, however, as one of valuation.

Lear, other than this, remains a play on global LVP, meaning the global Light Vehicle production, as well as a strong FX component. However, my stance as of June 2025, that Lear's revenue will grow in excess of global LVP because of its positioning for things that the automotive industry is spending more money on - such as seating, wiring systems (a necessity for modern tech), and automotive electrical systems. Lear has successfully positioned itself where it gets the advantage of the technological development in automotive development, while retaining exposure to legacy/analogue (seating), without having any major exposure to drivetrain/technology-dependent systems, such as ICE or EV.

This means that the company's growth rates are supported by a growing premium automotive segment, with increasing penetration of an EV sales share, trends that are confirmed by every major automotive out there. Lear, aside from this, has a clear history of R&D and innovation - it's the reason they continually get awarded contracts by major automotive players, and the fact that most automotive players are going to make, or already are making use of common platforms and automotive architecture, is going to make Lear's approach even easier and potentially more profitable.

There are risks to the company, of course, and we'll go through them - but I will state clearly that I do believe Lear has a considerable moat in its expertise area. Any LVP producer that works with Lear is unlikely to "easily switch" their manufacturer of premium seating or wiring/e-systems sourcing. This has to do with increased engineering costs, quality assurance, as well as the supply chains for moving, which, in the case of seating, are significant physical assets. This is not something or a segment that you just "go into".

I believe this, together with confirmed growth-over-market results in Q1 '25, confirms my positive thesis for Lear.

My reason for selling off much of my US-based positions was not based on a general dislike of the market, but rather a very unfavorable FX compared to my home currency. This has since normalized, and by rotating most of these, I avoided a 12-20% currency drop, which would have affected my positions the same way. The USDSEK pairing is, year to date, down 12.7%.

This also means that it's no longer a concern for investing in USD - the currency is now again "cheap" comparatively, at 9.66 SEK per USD (compared to over 11 SEK only 6 months back).

This is also why I am interested in Lear here.

Lear - Upside Is Possible, And In My View, Very Likely

Despite solid results, the market continues to view the risk side of Lear as opposed to the upside. Lear trades at a P/E of less than 7.6x, despite a forecasted EPS growth rate of 15% and a rating of BBB. The company has an extremely well-covered yield of 3.4%, and either beats or hits results more than 50% of the time historically. The average 5-year P/E valuation for Lear is double that of the current price/earnings multiple, and on a 20-year average, the company rarely goes below an 11.5x P/E.

In my last article, I cut my target to $150/share, citing tariff risks. I believe that is still a good price target (PT) for the company. I justify this using an 11.5x 2027E fair value of about $200/share, where an upside from $150/share would be 15% annualized or above. However, if we were to invest today, at less than $91/share, our upside is obviously very much superior to this.

To the fair value of $200/share, the company's upside is 38.6% per year, or 130%, which represents one of the highest upsides in automotive or automotive supplies/tech at this time.

Remember that for arguments against this, 11.5x is already a very bearish or conservative assumption for a company that in the past 10 years has actually traded significantly above that target, at closer to 15x. We're in an automotive slump, of that I see no doubt - but this does not mean that the companies are suddenly to be avoided. It means, in my approach, loading up on the quality players and never looking back.

Lear is such a quality player - and that's why I am long, and it's also why I keep "harping" about this thesis and about this company.

I see no valuation-related reason why Lear should be avoided here, and I believe that Lear is superior-valued and positioned even compared to a peer like Magna, which also has significant upside, better fundamental (A-rating, 5.21% yield), but comes In about 30% lower in total conservative RoR on a forward basis. That being said, I invest in both companies. Magna is the only NA peer that I would consider relevant in this context.

Q1 '25 is a confirmation of my positive long-term thesis. I look beyond 2025, and into 2026-2027, and I see a lot of things to like there.

Risks To My thesis

The primary risks to my thesis are related to recovery in LVP/automotives. While the industry is a highly competitive one, I believe major players like Lear and Magna have eked out positions that make the companies very hard to assail or compete with. However, their production is tied to cyclical trends, and when LVP production or demand goes down, as we're seeing now, this is what happens. However, I would actually say margin contraction has been less than what I expected for the company.

Secondary risks to consider are actually input costs. This is an industry that is dependent on a lot of copper and steel as well as energy, and I believe the pricing for these will continue to be volatile.

Beyond these twin risks, I don't see much that could de-rail Lear, and as long as we have automotives, I believe that Lear is not in a place where anything could derail it fundamentally.

My PT of $150/share remains - I see no reason to cut it, and my thesis for Lear is as follows.

Thesis

  • Lear is a fundamentally attractive business with an investment-grade credit rating, a solid albeit somewhat low yield, and a conservative upside if you manage to invest in the company at a relatively cheap valuation. We currently have more than an attractive valuation.
  • Given that the company has spent the last few years moving from a valuation of over 15-20x P/E to a valuation that now is at sub-8x P/E normalized, we're undoubtedly in a situation where the company could be considered undervalued, unless there's a significant risk of poor returns going forward.
  • I am maintaining my target for Lear at $150/share to account for the rising risks of tariffs, of margin impacts, of the company shifting production, and of the near term (3-4 years) not being as good to the company as current forecasts are expecting. The company, together with Magna International, represents a solid and long-term investment potential on my part, and I'm investing more now that the USD is appealing in terms of FX once more.
  • The company can be considered, after Q1 '25, cheap below $85/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a valuation target of $150/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of June 2025.

data.title: Murphy USA: Attractively Valued Thanks To Smart Capital Allocation
data.description:
The Murphy USA Investment Thesis

Murphy USA Inc. (NYSE:MUSA) is an overlooked investment because many stock screeners disregard slow-growing companies. But as the graph above shows, these companies can produce excellent results, leaving the S&P 500 (SPY) far behind.

The secret to the success of these under-the-radar companies is that they are often very shareholder-friendly and return a lot of capital to shareholders. In the case of Murphy, this is done through buybacks. And as long as Murphy continues to implement this strategy and buy back shares, I see no reason why it shouldn't be attractive to long-term investors. Plus, if the opportunity for multiple expansion arises, its potential increases even further.

But at the current price, which I consider fair, I don't see any potential for multiple expansion. So my thesis remains that share buybacks will promote EPS growth and are the most important point in the investment thesis.

What was my last thesis on MUSA about?

In October of last year, I wrote an article about Murphy, in which I expected that Murphy would continue to be a share cannibal. In this context, share cannibal refers to a company that aggressively repurchases its own shares and returns money to shareholders.

And that's exactly what Murphy did. They continued to hold on to their capital allocation strategy and repurchase shares.

However, I had one criticism at the time: I didn't see much room for multiple expansion, which made me feel like the upside was somewhat limited.

Since Murphy's share price has decreased since October, now is a good time to investigate why this happened and whether there's an opportunity for multiple expansion.

For this reason, I would now like to take another look at Murphy and see what has changed since then.

Murphy USA's Q1/2025 Results

The stock was sold off a bit after Murphy's Double Miss, which missed EPS targets by $1.33 and revenue targets by $160 million. The main reason for this was lower fuel prices, which resulted in lower revenue from petroleum products than in the previous year. Additionally, there were calendar effects that caused a volume decline.

Although expenses have also decreased compared to last year, unfortunately, these decreases could not offset the decrease in sales, resulting in a lower net income compared to last year.

However, it should be noted that store and other operating costs have only increased slightly, and costs for goods sold have even decreased, despite the fact that we are still in an environment where inflation is an issue.

Depending on how the situation in Israel/Iran develops and its potential impact on the oil price, it would not surprise me if we experience significant fluctuations in petroleum product sales in 2025. If the oil price were to rise slightly, the product sales would probably increase again. However, if the oil price were to rise too much, it would negatively impact sales. So this could therefore lead to temporary fluctuations.

MUSA's Balance Sheet

The cash position increased slightly from $47 million to $49 million in the last quarter. Similarly, inventories were reduced by nearly $37 million, but unfortunately, long-term debt increased by ~$142 million.

The main reason for this is the increased use of the revolving credit facility, which matures in 2026 and has a weighted interest rate of 6.59%. In this quarter, $200 million was borrowed, while at the end of last year, it was only $56 million. Therefore, I see the balance sheet as weaker than at the end of last year.

But overall, the balance sheet is still relatively secure, as I think that the outstanding liabilities are adequately covered by the net income.

MUSA's Capital Allocation

Murphy spent $151 million on stock buybacks, purchasing approximately 312k shares last quarter. This shows that Murphy's strategy continues to clearly focus on stock buybacks.

As the above diagram shows, these share buybacks actually reduce the outstanding share count and do not only serve to offset SBC. Therefore, I believe these stock buybacks positively affect shareholders.

The opening of new stores and further investments also seem to have a positive effect, as the ROIC stands at a strong 18%. However, it should be noted that ROICs were much higher in the past. So it is important to monitor how this develops in the future. If the ROIC continues to fall, it could be a warning sign.

MUSA Stock Valuation

Although Murphy's valuation remains above the five-year average of 13.9x, it has fallen from its five-year high of 22x to now 17x. The big question now is whether the normalized earnings are higher, which would make the multiple lower, or if the current earnings are representative.

If earnings were higher on a normalized basis, we could assume the potential for multiple expansion or rapidly increasing sales. However, since the situation is difficult to predict, I don't believe multiple expansion is likely.

For this reason, I currently consider the shares to be fairly valued, especially given that Alimentation Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) is trading at a very similar multiple.

Conclusion

I still see an intact investment case for Murphy. The company is aggressively buying back shares, and the entire business is likely to have a low single-digit revenue growth rate in the future. Many naturally wonder why a company with such low sales growth would be interesting. However, thanks to aggressive share buybacks, the company's EPS growth remains extremely strong, even though its sales growth is slow.

To illustrate, here are the two CAGRs for revenue and diluted EPS, to better understand the effect of buybacks. After all, stock prices often follow EPS development in the long term.

  • Revenue 3Y: 1.45%
  • Revenue 5Y: 7.69%
  • Revenue 10Y: 2.47%
  • EPS 3Y: 7.74%
  • EPS 5Y: 25.58%
  • EPS 10Y: 15.61%

Therefore, it is likely that EPS will still increase by a double-digit amount each year, even if Murphy's revenues only grow by 0-2% annually in the coming years. Therefore, I continue to view Murphy's stock as attractive, as the shareholder-friendly management is a huge success factor, and I don't see any signs that the stock buybacks will stop.

data.title: Remote Work, IoT, And Stock Repurchases Make Cable One A Buy
data.description:
Cable One, Inc. (NYSE:CABO) could receive more demand for its services after the deployment of the DOCSIS 4.0 along with the emergence of remote work and the IoT industry. Besides, in my opinion, the recent decrease in contract termination costs and potential decrease in taxes in the United States could represent stock price catalysts. Considering ongoing stock repurchases and the company's FCF generation, CABO appears to be trading substantially undervalued.

Cable One Trades At 1.32x FWD Cash Flow

Cable One, Inc. presents itself as a broadband communications company, offering services to more than 1 million residential and business customers in the United States.

The company operates in markets that are expected to grow in the coming years. According to market experts, the broadband services market is expected to grow at about 9.6% CAGR from 2024 to 2032. In addition, the television market size could grow at about 7.7% from 2025 to 2034. I used some of these assumptions in my financial model.

The Broadband Services Market, valued at USD 445.3 billion in 2023, is anticipated to reach USD 1,016.9 billion by 2032, expanding at a CAGR of 9.63% between 2024 and 2032. Source: Broadband Services Market.

Television Market Size was estimated at 396.88 (USD Billion) in 2024. Television Industry is expected to grow from 427.56 (USD Billion) in 2025 to 835.80 (USD Billion) by 2034. The Television Market CAGR (growth rate) is expected to be around 7.7% during the forecast period (2025 - 2034). Source: Television Market.

In the last 10-Q, the company did not report better than expected EPS GAAP. The EPS was close to $0.46, with quarterly revenue of $380 million. In the new quarter, the market expects EPS GAAP of $7.42 per share, and quarterly revenue of $380 million.

In 2025, investors are expecting significant EPS growth of 665%. The company appears to trade at 4.8x 2025 EPS, and 4.5x 2027 EPS. In my view, CABO trades undervalued at close to 1.32x FWD cash flow, and 5x FWD EBITDA. Peers trade at 7x FWD cash flow, and 8x FWD EBITDA.

Higher Speed, And DOCSIS 4.0

In my opinion, CABO will most likely offer better services thanks to higher access speeds. In the last quarter, the company noted that average monthly revenue per unit is usually associated with the company's Wi-Fi offerings, data usage, and broadband capacity. Given the company's recent investments to enhance fiber density, coverage, and network reliability, I believe that we could see further user growth and revenue growth in the coming years.

We continue to invest capital to, among other things, increase fiber density and coverage, expand our footprint, increase plant and data capacity, enhance network reliability and improve the customer experience. We have rolled out multi-Gigabit download data service to over 40% of our markets and currently offer Gigabit download data service to all of our passings. Source: 10-Q.

CABO also reported the deployment of DOCSIS 4.0, which is also expected to grow the company's revenue from business data and residential data, the company's most relevant sources of revenue. In this regard, CABO provided the following commentary in the last quarterly report.

We have also deployed DOCSIS 3.1 and begun the deployment of DOCSIS 4.0, which, together with Sparklight TV, further increases our network capacity and enables future growth in our residential data and business data product lines. Source: 10-Q.

Remote Work, And IoT

Among the reasons to expect user growth in the coming years, there is the growth in the number of individuals working from home.

According to Upwork, by 2025, an estimated 32.6 million Americans will be working remotely, which equates to about 22% of the workforce. Source: Forbes.

Remote work increased during the pandemic, and many individuals tasted the benefit of working from home. In my opinion, many others will try to work in the future, which could accelerate the demand for services offered by CABO.

In addition, I believe that the growth and recent investments in IoT could also improve the demand for broadband communications, WiFi, and other services offered by CABO. According to market experts, the IoT market is expected to grow at close to 24% CAGR from 2024 to 2032. CABO will most likely benefit from this new emerging market.

The global Internet of Things market size was valued at USD 595.73 billion in 2023. The market is projected to grow from USD 714.48 billion in 2024 to USD 4,062.34 billion by 2032, exhibiting a CAGR of 24.3% during the forecast period. Source: Internet of Things Market Size, Share, Growth, Trends, 2032.

Severance And Contract Termination Costs Declined In The Last Quarter

In the last quarter, CABO reported a large decrease in contract termination costs, which means that the number of employees will most likely not decrease in the coming quarters. In my view, the news is quite ideal. It means that directors inside CABO are expecting new projects. They need employees. With this in mind, we could expect revenue growth to remain positive in the future. I made such an assumption in my financial model.

Cable One Will Most Likely Benefit From Decreases In The Total Amount Of Taxes In The United States

For many years, CABO reported positive net income, and paid millions in taxes in the United States. With this in mind and recent information about potential decreases in taxes in the United States, in my opinion, the company is well positioned to benefit from new regulations.

As soon as new information about tax decreases arrives, I believe that analysts will most likely increase future free cash flow growth expectations. As a result, we could expect increases in the demand for the stock and perhaps stock price increases.

Stock Repurchases Could Bring Demand For The Stock

CABO has a share repurchase program, which initially included $450.0 million of share repurchases. In the last quarter, the company reported that it can acquire shares worth up to $143.1 million. Considering that CABO acquired a significant number of shares in the past and the current valuation of the stock, I would expect new stock repurchases at the current price mark.

The Company's Board authorized up to $450.0 million of share repurchases (with no cap as to the number of shares of common stock) under the Share Repurchase Program, which was announced on May 23, 2022. The authorization does not have an expiration date. The Company had $143.1 million of remaining share repurchase authorization under the Share Repurchase Program as of March 31, 2025. Source: 10-Q.

In the past, the company acquired 646,244 shares at close to $556.9 million, which means that CABO acquired shares at a price of more than $800 per share. Right now, we can buy shares in the market at less than $180 per share. It appears quite ideal.

Since the Company first became publicly traded in 2015 through March 31, 2025, the Company has repurchased 646,244 shares of its common stock at an aggregate cost of $556.9 million. The Company did not repurchase any of its common stock during the three months ended March 31, 2025 or 2024. Source: 10-Q.

Debt, And WACC

CABO appears to report a large amount of debt. However, I believe that the company knows well how to negotiate with debt holders out there. According to debt agreements that I could consult, some of the company's debt is linked to changes in the Secured Overnight Financing Rate plus a margin of about 1.25% to 1.75%. Given the company's debt agreements, I assumed a WACC of about 7%, which I believe is conservative. The following text about several debt instruments signed by CABO has been taken from the last quarterly report.

Under the Credit Agreement, the interest margins applicable to the Senior Credit Facilities are, at the Company's option, equal to either the Secured Overnight Financing Rate ("SOFR") or a base rate, plus an applicable margin equal to, (i) with respect to the Revolving Credit Facility, 1.25% to 1.75% plus a 10 basis point credit spread adjustment for SOFR loans and 0.25% to 0.75% for base rate loans, Source: 10-Q.

Risks

In my view, the largest risk for CABO comes from the total amount of debt and future negotiations with debt holders. In my opinion, debt holders may impose certain restrictions on the company in the form of covenants and other agreements. As a result, CABO may not be able to sign M&A agreements, or make large investments in new infrastructure. Under the worst-case scenario, CABO may not receive sufficient financing to further develop new networks, and revenue growth may be lower than expected.

Besides, I believe that increases in the interest rates could also lower the company's potential for net income growth and free cash flow growth. As a result, the company's net debt/EBITDA ratio could increase substantially, which may also lower the interest of certain investors. If the demand for the stock decreases, I would expect a decline in the price. In this regard, it is worth noting that CABO signed interest rate swap agreements to protect the company from large changes in the interest rates.

The Company is party to two interest rate swap agreements, designated as cash flow hedges, to manage the risk of fluctuations in interest rates on its variable rate SOFR debt. Source: 10-Q.

CABO operates in an industry that is regulated by the Federal Communications Commission and other entities. In the future, the FCC could block M&A deals, change the market share that players can control all over the country, and make decisions that may harm CABO's business model. I am not in a position to say whether the new regulations could benefit CABO, so I considered that new information about changes in the industry represents risks for the company.

Valuation

In the past, the company reported FCF of $400-$500 million. Hence, my free cash flow expectations include free cash flow of about $409 million in 2026, $550 million in 2031, and $662 million in 2035. I also included assumptions about future remote work, and the development of the IoT industry.

In addition, if we also include terminal FCF of 2.5x, net debt of $3.3 billion, 5.6 million shares, and a WACC of 7%, the implied valuation would stand at $195 per share.

My Final Words: Cabo Is A Buy At 1.32x FWD Cash Flow

CABO will most likely see an increase in its user base thanks to the deployment of DOCSIS 4.0 and further development of infrastructure to offer high-speed communications. I also believe that further expansion of remote workers and new development with regard to the IoT will most likely accelerate the demand for CABO's services. Besides, I believe that the recent decrease in contract termination costs, the potential decrease in taxes in the United States, and ongoing stock repurchases are good reasons to follow CABO. The company is clearly trading at very undervalued price marks. From here, CABO looks like a buy at 1.32x FWD cash flow.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Outsized Subscription Growth Is Enviable - Premium Valuations
data.description:
HIMS's Recovery Occurred Overly Fast & Furious - Reversing Momentum Likely Ahead

We previously covered Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) in March 2025, discussing how its steep correction had been well warranted, given the expensive valuations and the headwinds from the now available FDA-approved GLP-1 therapies.

While we had believed that its core personalized healthcare portfolio was likely to continue outperforming, as observed in the double digits non-GLP-1 revenue growth, the rich FY2024 performance metrics, and the management's promising FY2025 guidance, the stock's overly expensive valuations were undeniable then, worsened by the drastically higher short interest volume by +242.3% on a YoY basis.

HIMS 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Hold rating, HIMS has further retraced deeply by -37% nearer to our fair value estimates of $25.50 offered in our prior article indeed, thanks to the market wide meltdown in April 2025 with a similar pullback also observed in its obesity therapy/ GLP-1 peers in varying degrees.

Much of the headwinds are naturally attributed to the regulatory scrutiny observed in the healthcare provider/ insurance sector, worsened by the potential impacts from the "President Trump's new Executive Order that aims to drastically lower prescription drug prices for consumers."

This is on top of the ongoing Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services' pricing negotiations for Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Wegovy, Ozempic, and Rybelsus since early January 2025, as the new US administration also opts to not endorse the future Medicare coverage for obesity drugs as of early April 2025, with it naturally triggering headwinds to the GLP-1 sector as a whole.

On the other hand, thanks to the excellent dollar cost averaging opportunity, we are unsurprised that the bulls have already defended HIMS' floor at the $25s ranges during the worst of the April 2025 correction, as the stock rallies by +133.6% to retest the prior February heights in the $59s range by the time of writing.

Much of its tailwinds are naturally attributed to the increased availability of NVO's and Eli Lilly’s (LLY) GLP-1 offerings on the HIMS platform, as the latter continues to offer compounded formulations for "patients that are suffering from some type of intolerable dynamic where a commercial available dose is just not actually an option for them."

These naturally allow them to achieve the best of both worlds in GLP-1 related growth while complying with the "personalized 503A (regulation), ... we're talking about personalization of the [A facilities] (ph), that is regulatorily protected. So I think there's an alignment that both the regulation allows for."

As a result of these developments, we are unsurprised by HIM's double beat FQ1'25 performance, with total revenues of $586.01M (+21.7% QoQ/ +110.6% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 15.5% (+4.3 points QoQ/ +3.9 YoY/ +95.5 from FY2019 levels of -80%), and GAAP EPS of $0.20 (+81.8% QoQ/ +300% YoY).

Most importantly, its unique D2C and subscription approaches have paid off handsomely, as observed in the higher online revenues of $576.36M (+22.4% QoQ/ +115.2% YoY) thanks to the growing subscribers of 2.36M (+6.3% QoQ/ +38.8% YoY) and the expanding Monthly Online Revenue per Average Subscriber at $84 (+15% QoQ/ +52.7% YoY).

With 59.3% of its subscribers adopting the personalized solutions (+4.3 points QoQ/ +24.3 YoY), it is unsurprising that HIMS has rallied as it has, attributed to its ability to differentiate itself from other telehealth and legacy healthcare providers through the personalized solutions.

This is on top of the integration of its new laboratory diagnostics/ preventative testing, the enhanced fulfillment capabilities/ infrastructure, the expanded specialty offerings across obesity, trichology, sexual health (men/ female), mental health, and dermatology, and others:

Our vision involves expanding from hundreds of personalized treatments today to potentially thousands, powered by richer insights from lab diagnostics, growing subscriber data sets, and eventually daily tracking from wearable devices. (Seeking Alpha)

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why HIMS has felt confident to raise their FY2025 guidance, with revenues of $2.35B at the midpoint (+59.8% YoY) and adj EBITDA margins of 13.4% (+1.5 points YoY), with it building upon the robust FY2024 growth profile at +68.9% YoY and 11.9% (+6.3 points YoY), respectively.

This is on top of the healthcare company's ambitious 2030 target, with revenues of $6.5B (expanding at a CAGR of +28.1% from FY2024 levels) and adj EBITDA of $1.3B (expanding at a CAGR of +40.3%).

These reasons may also be why the consensus have already raised their forward estimates, with HIMS expected to chart an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +32%/ +34.4% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +14.7%/ +5.9% (a year ago), while building upon the 5Y historical top-line growth at a CAGR of +78%.

This is on top of the richer Free Cash Flow generation, the healthier balance sheet with a growing net cash position of $322.66M (+7.4% QoQ/ +58.4% YoY/ +434.5% from FY2019 levels of $60.36M), and the inherent lack of debts, with it underscoring how the growing adoption of HIMS' personalized healthcare offerings/ subscriptions have driven its growth opportunities.

HIMS Valuations

These reasons may also be why the market has upgraded HIMS' valuations to FWD EV/ EBITDA valuations of 41.13x and FWD P/E GAAP valuations to 85.99x, up from the recent bottom of 17.93x/ 37.90x, the 1Y mean of 27.32x/ 111.77x, and the 3Y mean of 29.16x/ NA, respectively.

The notable premium compared to the sector median of 11.90x/ 23.97x, respectively, implies that the stock's recent recovery has occurred overly fast and furious, especially when compared to its primary care legacy/ telehealth/ GLP-1 peers, including:

  • CVS Health Corporation (CVS) at FWD EV/ EBITDA of 9.59x,
  • Walgreens Boots Alliance, Inc. (WBA) at 11.45x,
  • Teladoc Health, Inc. (TDOC) at 5.78x,
  • UpHealth, Inc. (UPH) at 12.84x,
  • NVO at 13.72x, and
  • LLY at 29.03x.

This development implies that a moderate pullback to our Buy zones may be necessary for an improved return profile, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is HIMS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

HIMS 5Y Stock Price

For now, HIMS has already returned to its February 2025 heights as the stock runs away from its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages, albeit failing to break out of its prior resistance levels of $59s ranges.

Based on the management's raised FY2025 adj EBITDA guidance to $315M (+78% YoY) and the last reported shares outstanding of 246.61M, we are looking at an estimated FY2025 adj EBITDA of $1.27 per share (+73.9% YoY).

Combined with the higher 3Y EV/ EBITDA mean of 29.16x (previously at 20.91x), it is apparent that HIMS remains extremely expensive to our updated fair value estimates of $37.00.

Despite the raised consensus FY2027 adj EBITDA estimates to $523.48M and the resultant FY2027 adj EBITDA of $2.12 per share (expanding at a 2Y CAGR of +29.2%), it is undeniable that there remains a minimal margin of safety to our updated long-term price target of $61.80.

HIMS' Overbought Levels

The same overbought position has also been observed in HIMS' technical metrics, with the stock's inability to break out of the $59s ranges and the historical trends implying an ongoing reversal in its price trends (red oval).

Assuming that its historical trading trends persist, we are likely to see the stock retrace to its 100/ 200 day moving averages of between $42.50 and $32.80 in the intermediate term, with it implying a -36.4% downside at the midpoint from current levels.

The potential for volatility is observed in the drastically higher short interest volumes by +187.7% on a YoY basis as well, with any moderation in market sentiments from prior greed levels likely trigger a deep retracement from current levels.

As a result of the potential capital losses at current heights, we prefer to maintain our Hold rating for the HIMS stock.

Interested investors may want to wait for our Buy zones between the 100/ 200 day moving averages to materialize for an improved margin of safety. Despite the bright long-term prospects, some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Iberdrola: Stronger Conviction That This Is An Attractive Investment
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Iberdrola (OTCPK:IBDSF) in July 2024, in which I recommended a buy rating as I envisioned the world demanding way more power than it does today, and that will be a great demand tailwind for IBDSF, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I am now even more confident in the equity story. IBDSF is no longer just an energy producer, it is gradually becoming a low-volatility, infrastructure CAPEX-driven earnings grower.

Networks Become the Core Growth Engine

I have pointed out previously that IBDSF’s network business is a beneficiary of Europe’s grid modernization push, and I believe that narrative has become even more important since July last year. For a start, the 1Q25 results say it all: the regulated networks segment’s EBITDA grew 42.8% y/y to EUR2.42 billion and accounted for 52% of total group EBITDA. This is a very important shift that gives more confidence in the IBDSF earnings growth outlook as it fundamentally upgrades the quality of the IBDSF earnings base. As a recap, regulated networks operate under cost-plus tariff regimes; in exchange for this regulation, IBDSF has regional monopolies with predictable, inflation-linked returns that are not impacted by commodity price cycles.

The shift in earnings quality has a much more important implication than just earnings visibility. It also reduces the consolidated volatility in earnings from swings in power prices and renewables output (i.e., the generation and supply segment). More importantly, it significantly de-risks the equity story, as IBDSF is now seen as a much more stable company with visible earnings growth, and I think this justifies the case for a premium valuation multiple.

CAPEX Translates into Visible Earnings Growth

One of the risks I mentioned previously was that high capex needs may be a possible headwind for free cash flow (FCF). I no longer think that way. Management noted in the recent results that they plan to invest >EUR13 billion in networks between 2025 and 2026, and this is the important part: 90% of this is expected to be cash-generative within the first year. This is a very critical distinction, as it means this capex starts contributing to returns almost immediately, rather than years down the line (which is where the FCF gap may appear).
